name: kaizen
description: Guide for continuous improvement, error proofing, and standardization. Use this skill when the user wants to improve code quality, refactor, or discuss process improvements.
capabilities: Guide incremental code improvements following Kaizen principles
Apply Poka-Yoke error-proofing techniques to prevent bugs at design time
Enforce standardized coding patterns across the codebase
Implement just-in-time development to avoid over-engineering
Provide structured problem analysis using root cause methods
Support iterative refactoring with verification at each step
author: sickn33
securitySummary: This skill is a documentation-only guide for Kaizen software development methodology. Static analysis flagged 73 patterns in code examples within markdown documentation, but all findings are false positives. The backtick operators are TypeScript template literals (not shell commands), fetch calls are educational examples (not actual network requests), and environment variable references teach secure validation practices. No executable code exists in this skill.
valueStatement: Software teams struggle with inconsistent code quality and reactive error handling. This skill provides a structured Kaizen methodology for continuous improvement, error-proofing designs, and following standardized patterns.
userTitle: Apply Kaizen Methods for Code Quality
limitations: Focuses on process and methodology rather than domain-specific solutions
Requires team agreement on patterns for standardized work to be effective
Incremental approach may not suit urgent situations requiring immediate large changes
Does not provide automated tooling or linting rules directly

useCases.targetUser: Senior developers and tech leads conducting code reviews
useCases.title: Code Review Improvement
useCases.description: Apply Kaizen principles during code reviews to suggest small, incremental improvements rather than demanding complete rewrites. Each suggestion is verified and tested before moving to the next.

useCases.title: Error-Resistant API Design
useCases.description: Use Poka-Yoke techniques to design TypeScript APIs where invalid states are unrepresentable at compile time. Type system catches errors before runtime.

useCases.targetUser: Developers maintaining and modernizing legacy codebases
useCases.title: Refactoring Legacy Code
useCases.description: Apply iterative refinement approach: make it work, make it clear, make it efficient. Each iteration is complete and tested before proceeding to the next.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Code Improvement
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this function and suggest one small improvement following Kaizen principles. Explain why this change improves quality and verify it works before suggesting anything else.
promptTemplates.scenario: Quick iterative improvement during active development

promptTemplates.title: Error-Proof Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this API interface using Poka-Yoke principles. Show how to use TypeScript types to make invalid states unrepresentable. Provide before and after examples with type definitions.
promptTemplates.scenario: Designing new APIs or refactoring existing ones for safety

promptTemplates.title: Pattern Standardization
promptTemplates.prompt: I'm adding a new feature to this codebase. Analyze the existing code patterns for [feature type] and show me how to implement the new feature following the same standardized approach. Point to specific examples in the codebase.
promptTemplates.scenario: Maintaining consistency when adding new functionality

promptTemplates.title: YAGNI Refactoring
promptTemplates.prompt: Review this code for YAGNI violations. Identify any 'just in case' features, premature abstractions, or unused complexity. For each issue, show the simplest version that meets current requirements and explain what to delete.
promptTemplates.scenario: Cleaning up over-engineered code and reducing complexity

bestPractices: Make the smallest viable change that improves quality, verify it works, then proceed to the next improvement
Validate inputs and configuration at system boundaries, fail fast with clear error messages
Follow existing codebase patterns for consistency. Only introduce new patterns when significantly better and agreed by team
antiPatterns: Attempting large refactoring projects instead of incremental improvements
Building generic frameworks or abstractions before having multiple proven use cases
Adding 'just in case' features or optimizing without measuring actual performance issues

faq.question: When should I use Kaizen versus doing a complete rewrite?
faq.answer: Kaizen works for most improvements. Rewrites are rarely the answer. Break large problems into small, testable improvements. Each iteration should leave the code working and better than before.

faq.question: How do I apply Poka-Yoke in dynamically typed languages?
faq.answer: Use runtime validation at boundaries, assertion functions, and branded types. While not as strong as compile-time checking, early validation with clear errors still prevents many production issues.

faq.question: What if my team has no established patterns?
faq.answer: Start by documenting patterns you observe in working code. Propose standards based on existing successful patterns. Use linters and type checking to automate enforcement once agreed.

faq.question: How do I know if an abstraction is premature?
faq.answer: Wait for the Rule of Three: implement the same pattern three times in different places, then abstract. Duplication is cheaper than wrong abstraction. Abstraction should emerge from proven patterns.

faq.question: Can Kaizen work with tight deadlines?
faq.answer: Yes. Small improvements are faster than large rewrites. Focus on highest-impact changes first. The iterative approach reduces risk of broken code under deadline pressure.

faq.question: What tools support Kaizen methodology?
faq.answer: TypeScript for compile-time error prevention, ESLint/Prettier for standardized formatting, testing frameworks for verification at each iteration, and CI/CD for automated quality gates.
